answers.field_label: Please Enter Any Additional Planning Notes Here
answers.value:
We plan to return to pool training in a phased manner, starting with our Performance and Senior Development squads during the first two weeks, then gradually adding Age Group and Junior squads once we are confident that the new procedures are working smoothly. To reduce congestion, we will initially limit each swimmer to three in-pool sessions per week, combined with two dryland sessions delivered virtually via Zoom.

Lane assignments will be fixed by training group for the first month so that swimmers remain in consistent cohorts and we can simplify contact tracing if needed. We will maintain a maximum of four swimmers per 25m lane, with swimmers starting from opposite ends and designated resting spots marked on the pool deck. We have coordinated with the facility to stagger our arrival and departure times so that our swimmers are not overlapping with public swim blocks or other user groups.

We will also continue to use our existing WhatsApp group chats for quick day-of reminders about health screening and last‑minute schedule changes due to weather or facility issues. All coaches have reviewed the Return to Swimming Plan and have contributed feedback based on their experience from Phases 1 and 2 of our return, particularly regarding realistic warm‑up lengths, safe use of equipment (kickboards, pull buoys, snorkels), and how to manage younger swimmers who may need more reminders to follow distancing and traffic-flow rules.

Parents have already been surveyed about their comfort level with a February return, and the majority have indicated support as long as masking in common areas, symptom screening, and capacity limits remain in place. We will re‑survey families at the end of March to see if any adjustments to practice times, group sizes, or safety measures are needed. Finally, we are keeping a small contingency in our budget for additional PPE (extra masks, hand sanitizer, and disposable gloves for coaches) and for possible pool rental extensions if we need to split groups further to meet updated public health guidelines.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Describe How The Return To Swimming Plan Will Be Communicated To Your Club
answers.value:
The Return to Swimming Plan will be communicated to the entire Harbour City Aquatics membership using multiple channels so that families can review the information in the format that works best for them and can refer back to it easily.

1. **Email distribution of full plan and summary:**
   - We will send a detailed PDF of the full Return to Swimming Plan to all registered swimmers and parents using our TeamUnify mailing list (approximately 86 families). The email will also include a one‑page summary with key points: start dates by group, arrival and departure procedures, mask and distancing requirements, lane capacity limits, and what equipment swimmers must bring in their own mesh bags.
   - The email subject line will clearly state "IMPORTANT: Phase 3 Return to Pool Training Plan – PLEASE READ" so that it is not overlooked. We will send an initial email two weeks before the proposed start date (2026-02-01) and a reminder email three days before the first practice block (2026-02-12).

2. **Posting on club website and member portal:**
   - The complete plan and the one‑page infographic summary will be posted on our club website (www.harbourcityaquatics.ca) under a dedicated "Return to Pool" tab. The same documents will be uploaded to the TeamUnify documents section so that members can access them from the mobile app.
   - We will keep a version history so that if public health guidance changes, families can see when the plan was last updated and what sections were revised.

3. **Virtual town hall information session:**
   - One week prior to the return date, we will host a 60‑minute Zoom town hall meeting for all club members. During this session, I (Head Coach Dan McKenzie) and our COVID-19 Club Coordinator, Sarah Thompson, will walk through the plan slide by slide, including screenshots of the pool deck layout, entrance/exit routes, and sample daily health screening questions.
   - We will record the session and share the link with any families who cannot attend live. During the call, we will leave at least 20 minutes at the end for Q&A so that parents and swimmers can ask specific questions (for example, how inhalers are managed on deck, or what to do if a swimmer develops a cough during practice).

4. **Group‑specific follow‑ups by squad coaches:**
   - Each squad coach (Performance, Senior Development, Age Group, and Junior) will send a follow‑up email to their training group outlining the specific practice times, lane assignments, and any age‑appropriate reminders (e.g., extra guidance for younger swimmers on staying in their designated areas).
   - Coaches will also spend the first 10–15 minutes of the first two practices reviewing the main rules in person on deck, using simple language and visual cues (cones, arrows on the deck, and laminated signage) to reinforce the written plan.

5. **Visual aids and signage at the facility:**
   - We will place large laminated posters at the main entrance, in the lobby, and on the pool deck summarizing the return procedures and traffic flow. These posters will use clear icons and colour coding to indicate where swimmers should line up, where parents must stop, and which direction swimmers should walk.

6. **Ongoing reminders and updates:**
   - Any changes to the plan (e.g., modifications to cohort sizes or mask rules based on updated public health orders) will be communicated via email, posted on the website, and highlighted in our weekly club newsletter. We will also use our existing WhatsApp groups for each squad to send short, same‑day reminders about arrival times, screening, and equipment.

In all communications, we will emphasize that the plan is a living document that may evolve as Swim NS and provincial health authorities update their guidance, and we will clearly indicate whom to contact (myself or Sarah Thompson) with questions or concerns.

answers.field_label: Describe How Swimmers Parents Will Be Informed Of All Safety Precautions They Must Follow That Are Implemented By Your Club
answers.value:
Swimmers and parents will receive detailed, repeated, and age‑appropriate information about all safety precautions, with a focus on making expectations very clear before they arrive at the facility and reinforcing them consistently once training begins.

1. **Pre‑season safety information package (email and PDF):**
   - Along with the Return to Swimming Plan, families will receive a separate "Safety Precautions Handbook" as a PDF attachment. This handbook will spell out, in plain language, all club‑implemented precautions, including:
     - Daily self‑screening requirements before leaving home (temperature check, symptom checklist, and confirmation of no recent exposure to known COVID-19 cases).
     - Mask requirements for swimmers, parents, and coaches (where masks must be worn—lobby, changerooms, pool deck—and when they may be removed, i.e., immediately before entering the water and replaced as soon as the swimmer exits).
     - Physical distancing rules (2m spacing in lineups, on deck, and in the stands; designated waiting spots marked with tape; no congregating in changerooms).
     - Equipment policies (no sharing of personal equipment, swimmers must bring their own clearly labelled water bottle, kickboard, pull buoy, snorkel, fins, and mesh bag; club‑owned equipment used only by one cohort per session and disinfected between groups).
     - Spectator and parent access rules (parents not permitted on deck except for pre‑approved medical or accessibility reasons, drop‑off and pick‑up procedures, and designated waiting areas outside the facility).
     - Procedures for reporting illness or a positive COVID-19 test, including who to contact (COVID-19 Club Coordinator) and how return‑to‑play decisions will be made in line with public health guidance.

2. **Parent and swimmer orientation Zoom sessions:**
   - We will run two separate 45‑minute Zoom orientations: one aimed at parents/guardians and one tailored specifically to swimmers (split into older and younger groups). During the parent session, we will walk through:
     - A step‑by‑step "day in the life" of a practice under the new rules, from leaving home to returning after practice.
     - Examples of acceptable and unacceptable behaviour (e.g., no sharing towels, no high‑fives or hugs, staying in assigned lanes, and following coach instructions when resting at the wall).
     - What will happen if a swimmer forgets a mask or arrives late, and how we will handle minor rule breaches in a constructive way.
   - In the swimmer‑focused session, we will use slides with photos of the actual pool deck at Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre, showing where to line up, which arrows to follow, where to place bags, and how to rest at the wall while staying distanced.

3. **Written acknowledgement and consent:**
   - Before a swimmer can return to the pool, parents will be required to sign and return a digital acknowledgement form (via DocuSign) confirming that they have read and understood all safety precautions and agree to help their swimmer follow them.
   - The form will include specific checkboxes for key items (daily screening, masking, distancing, equipment rules, and reporting illness) so that parents are explicitly aware of each expectation.

4. **On‑site reinforcement with coaches and signage:**
   - During the first two weeks of training, coaches will allocate time at the beginning of every practice to review safety rules verbally, especially with younger groups. They will use the same language as in the handbook to keep messages consistent, and they will model correct behaviour (wearing masks on deck, maintaining distance when giving feedback, using hand sanitizer before and after adjusting lane ropes or equipment).
   - Clear signage provided by the facility and the club will be posted at all key decision points: entrance doors, changerooms, stairwells, and on deck. Signs will remind everyone about mask use, distancing, and hand hygiene, and will include arrows indicating one‑way traffic where applicable.

5. **Regular reminders via digital channels:**
   - Each Sunday evening, we will send out a short "Safety Snapshot" email summarizing any recurring issues from the previous week (for example, too many swimmers arriving more than 10 minutes early and congregating, or swimmers forgetting to bring filled water bottles) and gently reminding families of the correct procedures.
   - Coaches will also use squad WhatsApp groups to send quick reminders on practice days (e.g., "Don’t forget: masks on from the car to the pool deck, bring your own labelled water bottle, and complete the Kinduct health check before 3:00 pm").

6. **Use of the Kinduct online health monitoring platform:**
   - Swimmers and parents will be trained (via a short screen‑share demo during the parent Zoom session and a step‑by‑step PDF guide) on how to complete the daily health check in Kinduct. Completion of this screening will be a mandatory safety precaution before attending any practice.
   - The system will automatically flag any concerning responses for the COVID-19 Club Coordinator and head coach, who will then follow up with the family to review next steps (staying home, contacting public health, and timeline for returning to training).

7. **Two‑way communication and feedback:**
   - We will explicitly invite parents and swimmers to share concerns or suggestions about the safety precautions, either by emailing me directly (dan.mckenzie@harbourcityaquatics.ca) or by contacting our COVID-19 Club Coordinator. We will include this invitation, along with our phone contact details, in all major communications.
   - If we receive repeated questions or notice confusion about a particular rule (for example, whether swimmers can arrive in their suits or must use changerooms), we will update our written materials and highlight the clarification in the next weekly Safety Snapshot email.

By layering written materials, virtual orientations, required acknowledgements, on‑deck reinforcement, and regular reminders, we aim to ensure that both swimmers and parents fully understand not just what the safety precautions are, but why they are in place and how each family can help keep the entire club community safe.
